intro_text:
Platges de Comte is the plural-named beach on the west coast of Ibiza, in the municipality of Sant Josep de sa Talaia, about 8 kilometers west of Sant Agustí des Vedrà and a short drive south of Sant Antoni. The Catalan name is plural for a reason: it is not one beach but two adjoining sand coves on a low headland, Platja de Tramuntana facing north and Platja de Ponent facing west, separated by a rocky point that carries the beach's restaurant and facilities. In Spanish and in most tourist signage the same place is written Cala Comte or Cala Conta. The name traces to Nuño Sánchez, Count of Roussillon and a leader in the Christian conquest of Ibiza in 1234.

The water is the reason people come. The bay shelves gently over pale sand, and a steady flow of sea currents keeps fine sediment moving rather than settling, so the shallows hold a clear turquoise that grades to cobalt as the bottom drops away. This is not a marketing color: the EU bathing-water register classifies the site (station ES531M0481213) as "excellent," its highest band, and Cala Conta held a Blue Flag in 2010. The substrate is genuine sand rather than the pebble or rock that fills many Balearic coves, which is why the entry is comfortable and the swimming holds up over distance.

What closes the view is the offshore archipelago. Three protected islands stand on the western horizon directly off the coves: Sa Conillera, the largest, a mile long and topped by an 1857 lighthouse; S'Illa des Bosc; and the farther S'Espartar. All belong to the Es Vedrà, Es Vedranell i els Illots de Ponent nature reserve, declared in February 2002 under Balearic Decree 24/2002, a roughly 798-hectare protected area where landing on the islets is prohibited. The islets are a breeding ground for seabirds and birds of prey and form part of the EU Natura 2000 network.

Because the main cove faces due west and the islets sit on the sightline, Platges de Comte is one of the most-watched sunset positions on Ibiza. The sun drops behind Sa Conillera and its neighbors, the islands flatten into dark silhouettes, and the crowd that gathers on the sand is matched by boats anchoring offshore for the same view. The Sunset Ashram restaurant, on the headland between the two coves, plays DJ sets into the evening through the season. The beach has no entrance fee and limited formal parking that fills by late morning in July and August; Ibiza Airport is 16.5 kilometers away, roughly a 25-to-35-minute drive.
honest_reckoning_note:
The honest tension at Platges de Comte is that its draw and its strain come from the same fact: it is one of the best sunset positions on a small island that fills past capacity every summer. The two coves are modest, around 600 meters of frontage between them, and the formal parking is limited; in July and August it is gone by late morning, and the overflow parks along the access roads and walks in. The sand and the shallows that make the swimming good are also what the crowd comes for, and on a peak August evening the same west-facing line that gives the clean sunset draws hundreds of people onto a strip that was never sized for them.

The water quality is real and monitored, not theater: the EU register holds the "excellent" rating because currents flush the bay, not because anyone polished the description. But the islets that complete the view are a working nature reserve, not scenery, closed to landing and managed for breeding seabirds. The candid version of a visit here is that the beach delivers exactly what its pictures promise, turquoise water and a framed sunset, and that the cost of that is arriving early, accepting the crowd at golden hour, and treating the protected islands offshore as off-limits rather than a backdrop to land on.

day_in_time.dawn: At first light the two coves are quiet and the formal parking is empty. The water is flat and the turquoise is at its clearest before the wind builds, with the islets of Sa Conillera and S'Illa des Bosc sharp on the western horizon. This is the window before the late-morning fill, and the swimming over the pale sand is unhurried.
day_in_time.midday: By late morning the lots fill and the overflow parks along the access roads. The west and north coves both take full sun, the shallows read bright aqua, and the Sunset Ashram restaurant on the dividing headland runs at capacity. Boats anchor offshore through the afternoon, and shade on the sand itself is scarce.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon is the beach's signature hour. The sun lowers toward Sa Conillera, the islets flatten into silhouettes, and the crowd that gathers for the west-facing sunset is matched by anchored boats. The Sunset Ashram plays DJ sets into dusk; this is the most-photographed and most-crowded moment of the day.
day_in_time.night: After the sun drops behind the islets the sand empties quickly, the boats lift anchor, and the headland venue carries the evening. There is no resort behind the beach, so the coves go dark and residential once the sunset crowd disperses.

food_drink.name: Sunset Ashram
food_drink.description: The restaurant and bar on the rocky headland that divides the two coves, with panoramic west-facing views over the islets. It serves Mediterranean and Asian-fusion plates and runs DJ sets at sunset through the season; it is the beach's main on-site food and drink.
food_drink.where: On the headland between Platja de Ponent and Platja de Tramuntana

food_drink.name: Beach chiringuito fare
food_drink.description: Casual beach-bar food at the cove level: sandwiches, salads, cold drinks and beer, priced for a half-day beach visit rather than a destination meal. Seasonal, busiest at midday and at the sunset hour.
food_drink.where: At the Platges de Comte coves

food_drink.name: Sant Josep village dining
food_drink.description: Genuine Catalan-Mediterranean restaurants in the administrative town 7.4 kilometers inland, away from the beach crowd and at lower prices than the waterfront. The town also holds a weekly market useful for picnic supplies.
food_drink.where: Sant Josep de sa Talaia, the nearest town

timeline.title: Ibiza conquered; the count who names the beach
timeline.description: Ibiza is taken in the Christian conquest of 1234, in which Nuño Sánchez, Count of Roussillon, is a leading figure. The beach's name, Comte/Conta, derives from this count.

timeline.title: Sa Conillera lighthouse built
timeline.description: The lighthouse on Sa Conillera, the largest of the islets offshore from the coves, is built. It stands 20 meters tall at the island's northern end, about 60 meters above sea level, and was keeper-staffed until automation in 1971.

timeline.title: Sa Conillera lighthouse automated
timeline.description: The Sa Conillera lighthouse is automated and the keepers and their families leave the houses beside it, ending continuous human habitation of the offshore island.

timeline.title: Illots de Ponent declared a nature reserve
timeline.description: By Balearic Decree 24/2002, the islets off the coves, Sa Conillera, S'Illa des Bosc, S'Espartar and Ses Bledes, are protected within the Es Vedrà, Es Vedranell i els Illots de Ponent nature reserve, roughly 798 hectares, with landing on the islets prohibited.

timeline.title: Blue Flag awarded
timeline.description: Cala Conta is one of fifteen Ibiza beaches awarded the Blue Flag in 2010, recognizing water quality and beach management.

zones.name: Platja de Ponent (west cove)
zones.character: The west-facing sand cove and the beach's signature view. Shallow turquoise water over pale sand, looking directly out to the islets and the line of the sunset. The most crowded cove at golden hour.
zones.best_for: Sunset over the islets; swimming the clear shallows
zones.notes: West orientation puts the sun and the islets on the same sightline; this is the most-photographed position on the beach.

zones.name: Platja de Tramuntana (north cove)
zones.character: The north-facing sand cove adjoining Ponent across the headland. The same pale sand and clear water, but a different aspect and often a slightly different wind exposure.
zones.best_for: Quieter swimming away from the main sunset crush
zones.notes: Tramuntana means the north wind; this cove faces away from the direct west sunset.

zones.name: The dividing headland
zones.character: The low rocky point between the two coves, crowned by the beach's restaurant, shop and toilets, with panoramic views over both coves and the open water.
zones.best_for: Food, drink and the elevated sunset view
zones.notes: The Sunset Ashram restaurant occupies this point and runs DJ sets through the evening.

zones.name: Illots de Ponent (offshore reserve)
zones.character: Sa Conillera, S'Illa des Bosc and S'Espartar on the western horizon, the silhouettes that frame the sunset. Sa Conillera is the largest, a mile long with an 1857 lighthouse.
zones.notes: Protected since 2002 under Decree 24/2002; disembarking on the islets is prohibited and the area is a seabird breeding ground.

landmarks.name: Sa Conillera and its lighthouse
landmarks.description: The largest islet off the coves, about a mile long, the dominant shape on the western horizon. Its lighthouse, built in 1857 and 20 meters tall, sits roughly 60 meters above sea level at the island's north end; it was automated in 1971. Local legend places the birthplace of the Carthaginian general Hannibal on the island.

landmarks.name: Sunset Ashram
landmarks.description: The restaurant and bar on the rocky headland between the two coves, the beach's main venue, with panoramic west-facing views and DJ sets at sunset. Serves Mediterranean and Asian-fusion food.

landmarks.name: Illots de Ponent nature reserve
landmarks.description: The protected group of western islets, Sa Conillera, S'Illa des Bosc, S'Espartar and Ses Bledes, declared part of the Es Vedrà, Es Vedranell i els Illots de Ponent reserve in February 2002 under Decree 24/2002. Roughly 798 hectares (about 565 marine, 233 terrestrial), a Natura 2000 seabird breeding area closed to landing.

cultural_refs.title: Ibiza's most-watched sunset
cultural_refs.description: Platges de Comte is widely cited in Ibiza guides as one of the island's best and most popular sunset positions, owing to its due-west orientation and the islets that sit on the sightline. Crowds gather on the sand while boats anchor offshore for the same view.

cultural_refs.title: The Hannibal birthplace legend of Sa Conillera
cultural_refs.description: A local legend holds that the Carthaginian general Hannibal was born on Sa Conillera, in a house known as the Casa de n'Annibal on the south of the island. It is folklore rather than documented history, but it attaches to the largest of the islets that frame the beach's sunset.

recurring_events.name: Sunset DJ sets at the headland
recurring_events.when_text: Most evenings through the summer season, roughly May to October
recurring_events.description: The Sunset Ashram on the dividing headland runs rotating DJ sets timed to the west-facing sunset through the season, the recurring draw that fills the coves at golden hour.

businesses.name: Sunset Ashram
businesses.description: Restaurant and beach bar on the headland between the two coves, serving Mediterranean and Asian-fusion food with sunset DJ sets and panoramic west-facing views.

things_to_know.label: Two coves, one name
things_to_know.note: Platges de Comte is plural: Platja de Ponent (west-facing, the sunset cove) and Platja de Tramuntana (north-facing), split by a headland. Also written Cala Comte or Cala Conta.

things_to_know.label: Parking fills early
things_to_know.note: Formal parking is limited and gone by late morning in July and August; arrive before 10 AM or expect to park along the access road and walk in. Off-season parking is easy.

things_to_know.label: Sunset is the peak hour
things_to_know.note: The west orientation and the offshore islets make golden hour the busiest and most-photographed time; the crowd on the sand is matched by boats anchoring offshore.

things_to_know.label: The islets are off-limits
things_to_know.note: Sa Conillera, S'Illa des Bosc and S'Espartar are a protected reserve (Decree 24/2002); landing is prohibited and the area is a seabird breeding ground.

access_note: Platges de Comte is on Ibiza's west coast in Sant Josep de sa Talaia, about 16.5 km (25-35 min drive) from Ibiza Airport (IBZ) and 7.4 km from the town of Sant Josep, a short drive south of Sant Antoni. There is no entrance fee. Parking is free but limited and fills by late morning in summer; public transit to the cove is sparse, so most visitors drive.
spike_explainer:
The defining fact of Platges de Comte is the quality of the water and what stands beyond it. The shallows run a clear turquoise over pale sand, grading to cobalt where the bottom falls away, and that clarity is not seasonal luck. A steady flow of sea currents through the bay keeps fine sediment moving instead of settling, so the shallows stay transparent. The EU bathing-water register classifies the site, station ES531M0481213, as "excellent," its top band, and Cala Conta carried a Blue Flag in 2010. The substrate is genuine sand, not the pebble or rock common to Balearic coves, which is why the entry stays comfortable and the swimming holds over distance.

**Why the color holds.** Turquoise water needs three things at once: a pale reflective bottom, shallow depth, and clarity. Platges de Comte has all three. The sand is light, the bay shelves gently, and the current keeps the column free of suspended sediment. The predicted sand mineralogy here is a mixed quartz-feldspar-lithic regime, weighted toward quartz, which reads pale under sunlight. Where the bottom drops, the same clear water turns cobalt; the gradient between the two is the band every photograph of this beach is built on.

**The islets that close the view.** What separates Platges de Comte from a generic clear-water cove is the western horizon. Three islands stand directly off the coves: Sa Conillera, a mile long and the largest, topped by a lighthouse built in 1857; S'Illa des Bosc, nearer in; and S'Espartar, farther out. They are not decoration. Since February 2002, under Balearic Decree 24/2002, they have been a nature reserve, part of the Es Vedrà, Es Vedranell i els Illots de Ponent protected area of roughly 798 hectares, where landing is prohibited and the islets serve as a breeding ground for seabirds and birds of prey within the EU Natura 2000 network.

**The west-facing sunset.** The main cove, Platja de Ponent, faces due west, and the islets sit on that exact sightline. As the sun lowers it drops behind Sa Conillera and its neighbors, which flatten into hard dark silhouettes against the bright water, and the clear shallows in the foreground hold the last light. The geometry, west aspect plus offshore silhouettes plus a pale reflective bottom, is why guides rank this among the most-watched sunset positions on Ibiza, and why the crowd on the sand at golden hour is matched by boats anchoring offshore for the same frame.

**The two-cove structure.** The plural name is literal. Platges de Comte is two adjoining sand coves on a low headland, Platja de Ponent facing west and Platja de Tramuntana facing north, divided by a rocky point that carries the beach's restaurant and facilities. Tramuntana, named for the north wind, faces away from the direct sunset and tends to be the quieter swim; Ponent is the sunset cove and the crowded one. The split is why a single beach can offer both the signature golden-hour scene and a calmer alternative a few hundred meters around the point.

key_facts.label: Location
key_facts.value: Sant Josep de sa Talaia, west Ibiza

key_facts.label: Structure
key_facts.value: Two coves: Platja de Ponent (W) + Platja de Tramuntana (N)

key_facts.label: Offshore islets
key_facts.value: Sa Conillera, S'Illa des Bosc, S'Espartar

key_facts.label: Name origin
key_facts.value: Nuño Sánchez, Count of Roussillon (1234 conquest)

margin_notes.text: The turquoise is current-made: a constant flow of sea water through the bay keeps fine sediment in motion instead of settling, so the shallow column stays clear. The EU register holds the site at "excellent," its top band.

The name predates the tourism by eight centuries. Comte means count: Nuño Sánchez, Count of Roussillon, was a leader in the 1234 Christian conquest of Ibiza, and the beach carries his title in Catalan and Spanish alike.

The islets are a working reserve, not a backdrop. Since Decree 24/2002 the Illots de Ponent, about 798 hectares, are closed to landing and managed as a seabird and bird-of-prey breeding ground within the EU Natura 2000 network.

Sa Conillera, the largest islet, carries a 19th-century lighthouse and a tall tale: local legend names it the birthplace of the Carthaginian general Hannibal, in a house called the Casa de n'Annibal. Folklore, not record, but it sticks to the skyline.

Pale sand is half the optics. The predicted mineralogy here is a mixed quartz-feldspar-lithic sand weighted toward quartz, which reads light under sun; a bright bottom plus shallow clear water is what produces the turquoise, then cobalt where it deepens.

Ponent and Tramuntana are wind names: ponent the west wind, tramuntana the north. The two coves face the directions they are named for, which is why one frames the sunset and the other turns away from it.

reckoning_pullquote: The same west-facing line that gives the clean sunset draws hundreds of people onto a strip that was never sized for them.
